query: agentic AI claims
evidence: For GRC and compliance teams, agentic AI creates a hard evidence problem.  A policy may say human oversight. A platform may provide logs. A workflow may show approval events.  But can the organization prove lifecycle responsibility?  Can it show:  who authorized the action, what authority was delegated, which agent or tool acted, which human role accepted the outcome, what exception occurred, how remediation closed, and what evidence can be disclosed without violating privacy?  That is the gap I examine in this article.  My core claim:  Agentic AI auditability cannot be built on logs alone.  Logs are evidence ingredients. They are not automatically audit evidence chains.  The next phase of enterprise AI governance will require responsibility-linked evidence chains that connect intent, authority, tool action, outcome, exception, and closure.  This is not legal advice, certification, or an audit standard. It is a lifecycle evidence framework for professional discussion.  #GRC #AIComplian

evidence: Back in 2024, I started writing about AI and compliance, mostly to make sense of it for myself.  Eighteen months on, the FCA is letting firms test agentic AI live, with Barclays, UBS and Lloyds already taking part.  The technology question feels close to settled. The harder one isn't: Who is actually accountable for the AI?  That's less a technology problem than a people one, which is the part I find most interesting.  My new piece sets out five roles every AI team in financial services now needs: the Translator, the Sceptic, the Steward, the Builder and the Orchestrator.  One thing worth saying: these are roles to cover, not five separate hires. A good person can hold more than one. The real risk isn't a small team, it's when nobody is genuinely playing the Sceptic, or when the person who built the model is also the one signing it off as safe.  Full article below. evidence: Vector Similarity Search ​ Vector similarity search is a technique that finds items with similar meanings by comparing their numerical representations in high-dimensional space. Instead of matching exact keywords, it measures how close vectors are to each other using mathematical distance metrics. This allows systems to retrieve semantically related content even when the words are different. ​ Picture this: A warehouse where every item has a unique barcode that encodes its properties — size, weight, material, function. When you need a replacement part, the system does not just match the exact product code. It scans all barcodes and finds items with the most similar encoded properties, even if they come from different manufacturers or have different names. ​ This is exactly how AI systems remember and recall information.
